context.description: Philosophical prose on consciousness argues that a permanent lucid waking state could overload the brain and possibly lead to madness. It then asks how forgetting and language barriers prevent people from investigating or communicating altered states of awareness.
normalization_notes: Source language is German; summary translated into English.
Page is a prose book extract with reflective argument and section questions.

rows.paragraph_text: It asks whether a permanent lucid waking state would be sustainable for the brain and whether such a state would risk madness.
rows.text_content: It asks whether a permanent lucid waking state would be sustainable for the brain and whether such a state would risk madness.

rows.heading_text: How can we overcome language barriers?
rows.text_content: How can we overcome language barriers?

rows.paragraph_text: Forgetting is presented as the main barrier to studying elusive experiences, and the text asks whether they can be recorded externally and explained to others who may not recognize them as part of the self.
rows.text_content: Forgetting is presented as the main barrier to studying elusive experiences, and the text asks whether they can be recorded externally and explained to others who may not recognize them as part of the self.

rows.paragraph_text: It argues that different words, missing concepts, and the need to create new definitions force speakers into a closed vocabulary that makes communication with outsiders difficult.
rows.text_content: It argues that different words, missing concepts, and the need to create new definitions force speakers into a closed vocabulary that makes communication with outsiders difficult.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy philosophy_of_consciousness Philosophical book page on consciousness, forgetting, and language barriers. It questions whether permanent lucid waking is sustainable and whether altered experience can be studied or communicated at all.
